top_themes: Moral ambiguity in systems of power
Memory, loss, and the weight of the past
Survival and humanity at civilisational scale
The outsider who sees what insiders cannot

rating_calibration.notes: Generous rater — 4 stars is genuine enthusiasm, 5 stars is reserved for transformative works. Rarely rates below 3.

taste_clusters.name: Hard SF & Speculative Fiction
taste_clusters.description: Deep investment in science fiction that takes ideas seriously — especially first-contact, deep time, and civilisational stakes. Prefers biological/sociological SF over tech SF.

taste_clusters.name: Arthouse & Slow Cinema
taste_clusters.description: Patient, visually precise filmmaking where mood and implication do the work of dialogue. Tarkovsky, Bergman, and their successors — plus contemporary directors who inherited that tradition.

taste_clusters.name: Literary Crime & Moral Complexity
taste_clusters.description: Crime as a vehicle for character study and institutional critique rather than plot mechanics. Breaking Bad, The Wire, No Country for Old Men — the kind of work where the criminal is a mirror.

taste_clusters.name: Soul, Jazz & Melancholic Atmospherics
taste_clusters.description: Music that sits still and breathes. Nina Simone, Tom Waits, Portishead, Boards of Canada — emotional precision over energy, melancholy as aesthetic choice.

dislikes_pattern: Formulaic genre fiction without thematic ambition (airport thrillers, most superhero films after 2015)
Comedy that relies on embarrassment or cruelty rather than wit
Exposition-heavy storytelling that explains what it should show
High-concept SF that uses ideas as decoration rather than investigation

raw_response.profile_summary: Your taste converges on a single obsession: serious ideas rendered in beautiful form. Whether it's hard SF that takes evolutionary biology as seriously as Tchaikovsky does, slow cinema that trusts the audience to sit with unease, or jazz that turns sadness into architecture — you want work that believes in itself. You rate 5 stars rarely and precisely. The canon you're building is characterised by moral complexity, patient pacing, and a preference for ambiguity over resolution. You consume across every medium but with consistent depth rather than breadth — finishing series, re-reading novels, returning to albums. The recurring motif across your highest-rated work is the outsider who sees clearly because they're not invested in the system's self-image.

youtube_chapters.name: Pandemic Knowledge Binge
youtube_chapters.summary: March 2020. Nowhere to go. Kurzgesagt, Veritasium and Philosophy Tube became the curriculum nobody signed up for. The algorithm delivered weeks of cosmology, epistemology and explainer videos on things I'd never thought to wonder about. Twelve-minute videos on black dwarfs. Forty-minute essays on free will. The pandemic was terrible but the YouTube backlog was genuinely educational.

youtube_chapters.name: Analog Sound Rabbit Hole
youtube_chapters.summary: Started with a Nina Simone live concert on YouTube. Ended three months later deep in vinyl restoration tutorials, Hi-Fi amplifier physics, and My Analog Journal. Bought a turntable in November 2021. Now maintain an opinion about moving coil cartridges. This is a YouTube-specific danger.

youtube_chapters.name: Film Language Phase
youtube_chapters.summary: Every Frame a Painting archives in one sitting. Then scene-by-scene breakdowns of Villeneuve, Tarkovsky, and Kubrick. Tom Scott's editing style analysis. Fell into video essays about cinematography, sound design, and what directors are actually saying when they choose a lens or an angle. Cinema stopped being passive.

youtube_chapters.name: Architecture & The City
youtube_chapters.summary: Tom Scott videos about weird UK infrastructure led to architecture channels. Brutalism, Bauhaus, Berlin housing estates, the urban planning failures of the 70s. Somehow ended up watching six hours of Jane Jacobs content. Philosophy Tube's gentrification essay remains one of the best things I've watched.

recommendations.title: The Power of the Dog
recommendations.reason: Your highest-rated films combine psychological tension with landscape and restraint. Campion uses the American West the way Tarkovsky uses nature — as a force that reveals character. Benedict Cumberbatch's performance is in the register of Joaquin Phoenix in The Master, which you rated 5 stars.
recommendations.potential_issue: Slow build — nothing happens for 40 minutes in conventional plot terms. That's a feature, not a bug, given your viewing pattern.

recommendations.title: Shogun
recommendations.reason: Sits at the intersection of your two strongest TV clusters: patient political drama (Succession, Slow Horses) and historical epic (Chernobyl, Andor). The 2024 remake strips out the Western-gaze problems of the original and treats both cultures as equally complex. The subtitling choice alone is worth a 5-star rating for craft.
recommendations.potential_issue: 10 episodes. Comparable commitment to Chernobyl, which you completed and rated 5 stars.

recommendations.title: A Memory Called Empire
recommendations.reason: First-contact diplomatic SF that reads like Le Guin — but grounded in Aztec archaeology and Byzantine history. Your top-rated SF books all take cultural contact seriously (Children of Time, Hyperion). This one takes it most seriously of all. The protagonist is a historian navigating an empire that wants to consume her culture, which matches your recurring taste for outsider-sees-clearly protagonists.
recommendations.potential_issue: Book one of two. Book two (A Desolation Called Peace) is also excellent.

recommendations.title: The Name of the Wind
recommendations.reason: Your fantasy reading clusters around prose quality and character interiority (Lies of Locke Lamora, Piranesi, Good Omens). Rothfuss writes fantasy as literature — the frame narrative is clever, the magic system is the best in the genre, and Kvothe is unreliable in exactly the way that rewards careful reading. You'll finish it in a weekend.
recommendations.potential_issue: Book three is unfinished and may never arrive. Books one and two still stand alone as complete stories.

recommendations.title: Aftersun
recommendations.reason: A24 slow cinema that uses memory and perspective with the same structural sophistication as Mulholland Drive, but in an intimate domestic register. The ending is one of the best-crafted ambiguous moments in recent cinema — right in your zone of appreciation for films that trust the audience to supply meaning.
recommendations.potential_issue: Extremely quiet. No plot in a conventional sense. Very high ceiling, very demanding.

recommendations.title: Station Eleven
recommendations.reason: Post-pandemic ensemble drama built around a Shakespeare travelling company. Matches your dual taste in literary SF (Station Eleven the book) and prestige TV character work (The Leftovers is the closest reference point — same showrunner energy). Non-linear, emotionally precise, not interested in plot mechanics.
recommendations.potential_issue: The pandemic framing hit differently pre-2020 than it does now. Intentionally disorienting timeline.
